content:
===BEGIN===
Encryption secures online messages by transforming plaintext into ciphertext through mathematically rigorous algorithms, ensuring confidentiality against adversaries. Modern protocols predominantly employ asymmetric cryptography for key exchange and symmetric cryptography for bulk message encryption. Initially, a sender and receiver use asymmetric algorithms—such as RSA or Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman—to securely negotiate a shared session key over an insecure channel. This session key then encrypts the message with symmetric ciphers like AES, which provide computational efficiency and resistance to cryptanalysis. The security hinges on the infeasibility of deriving the session key from intercepted ciphertext without the private keys, grounded in hard mathematical problems like integer factorization or discrete logarithms. Additionally, message integrity and authenticity are maintained by cryptographic hash functions and digital signatures, preventing tampering and impersonation. Combined, these mechanisms enforce end-to-end confidentiality and trust, even when communication traverses untrusted networks, preserving privacy in real-time digital interactions.
